title: [tyrantrum] Battle Guide
summary: A [rock]/[dragon] physical breaker built around [head-smash] pressure and a 1.5x [strong-jaw] boost on [crunch] and fire-fang.

Ground, Steel, Ice, [dragon], and Fairy all land for 2x

bodyMarkdown:
## Overview
[tyrantrum] is a [rock]/[dragon] physical breaker whose entire gameplay loop hinges on [head-smash] recoil damage and the [strong-jaw] boost on its biting coverage. With base Attack 121 and Def 119, the raw stat distribution is closer to an old-school wallbreaker than a modern sweeper — it wants one setup turn, and then it wants to swing for the fences.

## Strengths
An [adamant]-natured base 121 Attack turns [head-smash] into a genuine one-shot tool against [flying], Fire, and Ice cores that most [rock] types can't punish cleanly. The [dragon] typing on its second STAB means [dragon-claw] also threatens [dragon] mirrors without recoil, and [strong-jaw] applies a 1.5x multiplier to [crunch], turning it into a functional Dark STAB that eats Ghost and Psychic pivots. The 119 physical Def and 82 HP give [tyrantrum] one free physical tank, which is usually the setup window for [dragon-dance].

## Weaknesses & Answers
The 4x Fighting weakness dominates everything. A single close-combat or drain-punch from a priority-less attacker still almost always finishes [tyrantrum] off, and any unresisted Fighting move ends the sequence. Ground, Steel, Ice, [dragon], and Fairy all hit for 2x, so the list of safe switch-ins is short. Base 71 Speed is the structural problem — without a [dragon-dance] boost [tyrantrum] loses to nearly every offensive answer. Priority alone (mach-punch, extreme-speed) bypasses the setup entirely, and the [head-smash] recoil combined with 59 SpD means long games are simply unwinnable.

## Recommended Moveset
[head-smash] is the STAB main. Its recoil is real, but nothing else on the learnset reproduces that damage ceiling. [dragon-claw] is the second STAB, picked specifically because it's recoil-free and keeps [tyrantrum] alive across multiple attacks. [crunch] is the [strong-jaw]-boosted coverage that punishes Ghost and Psychic switch-ins — it functionally replaces a dedicated Dark STAB. [dragon-dance] is the fourth slot because 71 Speed is simply too slow untreated. If the team already provides Speed control, earthquake is the alternative coverage for Steel and Fire pivots.

## Item · Ability · Tera
[focus-sash] is the default item, banking one hit survived so [tyrantrum] can either swing back or set up. [choice-scarf] is the faster alternative when [dragon-dance] setup opportunities aren't realistic — it turns base 71 into immediate Speed-tier pressure at the cost of locking into [head-smash]. The ability must be [strong-jaw] — [rock]-head is only a recoil fix and costs the biting multiplier. Tera [dragon] maxes [dragon-claw] and outrage damage, and Tera [flying] is the dedicated answer to the 4x Fighting weakness, flipping the most dangerous matchup into a neutral one.

## Teammates & Synergy
A Steel or Poison pivot to absorb Fairy moves is mandatory, as is a Fighting-resist answer (Psychic or Fairy ally). A stealth-[rock] lead makes [tyrantrum]'s damage ceiling much more reliable, turning 2HKOs into OHKOs against [flying] and Fire pivots. Finally, an intimidate user or fake-out disruptor buys the [dragon-dance] setup turn against faster offensive teams.

## Play Patterns
Early turns are for pressure reads, not direct damage. Only click [head-smash] when the opponent cannot bring in a Fighting revenge killer, and only use [dragon-dance] behind a switch or protect cover. Save Tera [flying] for the moment a close-combat is telegraphed — that single flip is often the difference between winning and losing the match. [tyrantrum] is a one-setup, two-swing card; overstaying is how it loses.
